expanded_description: Understand how differences in electronegativity between bonded atoms create polar and nonpolar covalent bonds, and learn to predict bond polarity, molecular polarity, and their effects on chemical behavior.

core_concept.explanation: Bond polarity arises from unequal sharing of electrons between atoms with different electronegativities. The greater the electronegativity difference ($\Delta\chi$), the more polar the bond. This creates partial charges ($\delta+$ and $\delta-$) that influence molecular properties, intermolecular forces, and chemical reactivity.

image.alt: Diagram showing the continuum from nonpolar covalent to ionic bonding
image.caption: Figure 6.6.1: Bond polarity continuum—electronegativity difference determines bond character from nonpolar covalent ($\Delta\chi < 0.4$) to polar covalent ($0.4 \leq \Delta\chi < 1.7$) to ionic ($\Delta\chi \geq 1.7$).

sections.introduction: Electronegativity is the measure of an atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a covalent bond. When two atoms with different electronegativities bond, electrons are pulled closer to the more electronegative atom, creating an uneven distribution of charge.

key_concept.definition: Electronegativity ($\chi$) is a dimensionless quantity that describes an atom's tendency to attract bonding electrons. The Pauling scale is most commonly used, with fluorine having the highest value ($\chi = 4.0$).
key_concept.context: Electronegativity increases across a period (left to right) and decreases down a group in the periodic table.

classifications.behavior: Atoms in the upper right corner (excluding noble gases) have highest electronegativity; atoms in lower left have lowest.

examples.explanation: Electronegativity decreases: F (4.0) > O (3.5) > N (3.0) > C (2.5) across Period 2.
Electronegativity decreases down Group 17: F (4.0) > Cl (3.0) > Br (2.8) > I (2.5).

classifications.behavior: The electronegativity difference determines how equally electrons are shared between bonded atoms.

examples.explanation: Identical atoms share electrons equally; pure nonpolar covalent bond.
Different electronegativities create polar covalent bond with partial charges.
Large electronegativity difference results in electron transfer; ionic bond.

principles.statement: Bond polarity is quantified by $\Delta\chi = |\chi_A - \chi_B|$, where $\chi_A$ and $\chi_B$ are the electronegativities of the bonded atoms.
principles.application: For $\text{H}-\text{F}$: $\Delta\chi = |2.1 - 4.0| = 1.9$, indicating a highly polar covalent bond approaching ionic character.
principles.analogy: Like a tug-of-war: the stronger team (more electronegative atom) pulls the rope (electron pair) closer to their side.

sections.introduction: When electrons are unequally shared in a polar covalent bond, the more electronegative atom acquires a partial negative charge ($\delta-$) while the less electronegative atom acquires a partial positive charge ($\delta+$). This charge separation creates a dipole moment.

key_concept.definition: A dipole moment ($\mu$) measures the magnitude of charge separation in a bond or molecule: $\mu = Q \times r$, where $Q$ is the magnitude of partial charge and $r$ is the distance between charges.
key_concept.context: Dipole moments are measured in Debye (D); 1 D = $3.34 \times 10^{-30}$ C·m.

components.interpretation: Larger charge separation or longer bond length increases dipole moment magnitude.

image.alt: Diagram showing dipole moment vector in a polar bond
image.caption: Figure 6.6.2: Dipole moment vector points from positive ($\delta+$) to negative ($\delta-$) end of the bond.

categories.description: Partial charges are denoted by $\delta+$ (partial positive) and $\delta-$ (partial negative) to indicate incomplete charge separation.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Unlike full ionic charges ($+1$, $-1$, etc.), partial charges represent fractional electron density shifts, typically $0.1$ to $0.5$ elementary charge units.

examples.explanation: In HF, fluorine attracts electrons strongly, creating $\text{H}^{\delta+}$ and $\text{F}^{\delta-}$ with $\delta \approx 0.4$.

image.caption: Partial charge distribution in hydrogen fluoride.

examples.explanation: In carbonyl groups, oxygen is $\delta-$ and carbon is $\delta+$, making carbon electrophilic.

categories.description: Bond dipoles are represented as vectors pointing from the positive end to the negative end of the bond.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Vector notation helps visualize and calculate net molecular dipole moments by summing individual bond dipoles.

examples.explanation: Vector points from H ($\delta+$) to Cl ($\delta-$); magnitude $\mu = 1.08$ D.
Strong dipole ($\mu \approx 2.4$ D) points from C to O; important for carbonyl reactivity.

categories.description: Dipole moment depends on both electronegativity difference and bond length.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Larger $\Delta\chi$ increases charge separation ($Q$); longer bonds increase distance ($r$); both increase $\mu$.

examples.explanation: HF: $\Delta\chi = 1.9$, $\mu = 1.91$ D; HI: $\Delta\chi = 0.4$, $\mu = 0.44$ D. Larger $\Delta\chi$ dominates.

principles.statement: By convention, the dipole moment vector points from the positive end ($\delta+$) to the negative end ($\delta-$) of the bond, opposite to electron displacement direction.
principles.application: In $\text{H}-\text{Cl}$, the vector points from H to Cl, indicating Cl is the negative end despite electrons moving toward Cl.
principles.analogy: Like an arrow showing where the 'weight' is concentrated—the arrowhead points to the heavier (more negative) side.

sections.introduction: A critical distinction: individual bonds may be polar, but the overall molecule may be nonpolar if bond dipoles cancel due to symmetric geometry. Molecular polarity depends on both bond polarity AND molecular geometry.

key_concept.definition: Molecular polarity is determined by the vector sum of all bond dipoles in a molecule. If the sum equals zero, the molecule is nonpolar; if nonzero, the molecule is polar.
key_concept.context: VSEPR geometry predictions (Section 6.5) are essential for determining molecular polarity.

classifications.behavior: Nonpolar molecules have no permanent dipole; they interact primarily through London dispersion forces.

examples.explanation: Two polar $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bonds point in opposite directions ($180^\circ$); dipoles cancel exactly.

image.caption: Opposing dipoles cancel in linear geometry.

examples.explanation: Four polar $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bonds arranged symmetrically; vector sum = 0.
Three polar $\text{B}-\text{F}$ bonds at $120^\circ$; dipoles cancel in plane.

characteristics: At least one polar bond present
classifications.behavior: Polar molecules have permanent dipoles; they exhibit dipole-dipole interactions and often hydrogen bonding.

examples.explanation: Two polar $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bonds at $104.5^\circ$; dipoles add to give $\mu = 1.85$ D.
Three polar $\text{N}-\text{H}$ bonds with lone pair; dipoles add to give $\mu = 1.47$ D.
Four bonds but different atoms; $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ dipole not cancelled by $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds.

principles.statement: Highly symmetric molecular geometries (linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, trigonal bipyramidal, octahedral) with identical terminal atoms produce nonpolar molecules even with polar bonds.
principles.application: $\text{SF}_6$ (octahedral, all F) is nonpolar; replace one F with Cl to make $\text{SF}_5\text{Cl}$, and it becomes polar due to broken symmetry.
principles.analogy: Like a balanced mobile: if weights are evenly distributed (symmetric), it stays level (nonpolar); if unbalanced, it tilts (polar).

principles.statement: Lone pairs on the central atom often create asymmetry, making molecules polar even when all bonds are identical.
principles.application: $\text{CH}_4$ (no lone pairs) is nonpolar; $\text{NH}_3$ (1 lone pair) is polar; $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (2 lone pairs) is more polar.
principles.example: Lone pairs contribute to molecular dipole by creating electron density on one side of the molecule.

sections.section_title: Effects of Polarity on Physical and Chemical Properties
sections.introduction: Bond and molecular polarity profoundly influence intermolecular forces, which determine physical properties like boiling point, melting point, solubility, and viscosity, as well as chemical reactivity patterns.

key_concept.definition: Polarity affects how molecules interact with each other and with external fields. Polar molecules experience stronger intermolecular attractions and respond to electric fields.
key_concept.context: Understanding polarity effects enables prediction of solubility ('like dissolves like'), boiling points, and reaction mechanisms.

categories.description: Polarity determines the type and strength of intermolecular forces between molecules.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Nonpolar molecules: London dispersion forces only; Polar molecules: dipole-dipole interactions plus dispersion; H-bonding molecules: strongest dipole interactions.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_4$ (nonpolar): bp $-161^\circ\text{C}$; $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$ (polar): bp $-24^\circ\text{C}$. Polarity increases bp by $137^\circ\text{C}$.
$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (H-bonding): bp $100^\circ\text{C}$; $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ (dipole-dipole): bp $-60^\circ\text{C}$. H-bonding dramatically increases bp.

image.caption: Hydrogen bonding effect on boiling point.

categories.description: The 'like dissolves like' rule: polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents; nonpolar solutes dissolve in nonpolar solvents.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Dissolution requires solute-solvent interactions to overcome solute-solute and solvent-solvent interactions; similar polarity enables favorable interactions.

examples.explanation: Ionic $\text{NaCl}$ dissolves in polar water; ion-dipole interactions stabilize dissolved ions.
Nonpolar oil does not dissolve in polar water; oil-water interactions weaker than water-water H-bonds.

categories.description: Polar bonds create sites of partial charge that serve as targets for nucleophilic or electrophilic attack in chemical reactions.
categories.detailed_mechanism: $\delta+$ atoms are electrophilic (attract electron-rich species); $\delta-$ atoms are nucleophilic (attract electron-poor species).

examples.explanation: $\text{C}^{\delta+}=\text{O}^{\delta-}$ makes carbon electrophilic; nucleophiles attack carbon in addition reactions.
$\text{H}^{\delta+}-\text{Cl}^{\delta-}$ makes H acidic; $\text{H}^+$ readily donated to bases.

categories.description: Polar molecules align with external electric fields due to their permanent dipole moments.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Electric field exerts torque on molecular dipoles, rotating them to align $\delta-$ end toward positive plate and $\delta+$ end toward negative plate.

examples.explanation: Charged rod deflects flowing water stream; polar water molecules align with the electric field.

image.alt: Water stream deflected by charged rod
image.caption: Polar molecules respond to electric fields.

principles.statement: Substances with similar polarity mix well; substances with different polarity tend to separate into distinct phases.
principles.application: Ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$, polar) mixes with water; hexane ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}$, nonpolar) does not.
principles.analogy: Like social groups: similar people (polarities) interact comfortably; very different people tend to stay in separate groups.

sections.introduction: Apply electronegativity values, molecular geometry, and dipole analysis to predict bond polarity, molecular polarity, and related properties.

worked_examples.title: Determining Bond Polarity
worked_examples.problem: Rank the following bonds in order of increasing polarity: $\text{C}-\text{H}$, $\text{N}-\text{H}$, $\text{O}-\text{H}$, $\text{F}-\text{H}$. Use electronegativity values: $\chi_\text{H}=2.1$, $\chi_\text{C}=2.5$, $\chi_\text{N}=3.0$, $\chi_\text{O}=3.5$, $\chi_\text{F}=4.0$.

step_by_step.explanation: Larger $\Delta\chi$ corresponds to greater bond polarity.
$\text{C}-\text{H}$ is nearly nonpolar; $\text{F}-\text{H}$ is highly polar.

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasing polarity: $\text{C}-\text{H} < \text{N}-\text{H} < \text{O}-\text{H} < \text{F}-\text{H}$. The $\text{F}-\text{H}$ bond is most polar due to fluorine's highest electronegativity.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Molecular Polarity
worked_examples.problem: Determine whether $\text{SO}_2$ and $\text{SO}_3$ are polar or nonpolar. Explain your reasoning.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{SO}_2$: S central, 2 bonds to O, 1 lone pair on S. $\text{SO}_3$: S central, 3 bonds to O, 0 lone pairs on S.
$\text{SO}_2$: 2 bonding + 1 lone pair = bent geometry. $\text{SO}_3$: 3 bonding + 0 lone pairs = trigonal planar.
S-O bonds are polar ($\Delta\chi = 3.5 - 2.5 = 1.0$); both molecules have polar bonds.
$\text{SO}_2$: bent geometry prevents dipole cancellation → polar. $\text{SO}_3$: trigonal planar symmetry cancels dipoles → nonpolar.

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{SO}_2$ is polar (bent geometry, dipoles don't cancel); $\text{SO}_3$ is nonpolar (trigonal planar symmetry, dipoles cancel). Both have polar bonds, but geometry determines molecular polarity.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Boiling Points
worked_examples.problem: Arrange the following compounds in order of increasing boiling point: $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$, $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$. Explain based on polarity and intermolecular forces.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{CH}_4$: nonpolar (symmetric tetrahedral); $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$: polar (asymmetric); $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$: polar with H-bonding.
$\text{CH}_4$: London dispersion only; $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$: dipole-dipole + dispersion; $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$: H-bonding + dipole-dipole + dispersion.
Stronger intermolecular forces require more energy to overcome → higher boiling point.

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasing boiling point: $\text{CH}_4 < \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} < \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$. $\text{CH}_4$ has only weak dispersion forces; $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$ has stronger dipole-dipole interactions; $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ has the strongest hydrogen bonding, requiring the most energy to vaporize.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Polarity, Intermolecular Forces, and Physical Properties

worked_examples.title: Solubility Prediction
worked_examples.problem: Predict whether each substance will dissolve in water: (a) $\text{NH}_3$, (b) $\text{CCl}_4$, (c) $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ (acetic acid). Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: Water is highly polar with hydrogen bonding capability; it dissolves polar and H-bonding substances.
Ammonia is polar and can H-bond with water (N-H···O); should be soluble.
Carbon tetrachloride is nonpolar (symmetric tetrahedral); cannot H-bond; should be insoluble.
Acetic acid is polar with -OH group for H-bonding; should be highly soluble.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{NH}_3$: soluble (polar, H-bonding); (b) $\text{CCl}_4$: insoluble (nonpolar); (c) $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$: soluble (polar, H-bonding). The 'like dissolves like' rule correctly predicts solubility based on polarity matching with water.

summary: Bond polarity arises from electronegativity differences between bonded atoms, creating partial charges ($\delta+$ and $\delta-$) and bond dipole moments. Molecular polarity depends on both bond polarity AND molecular geometry—symmetric arrangements of polar bonds can cancel to produce nonpolar molecules. Polarity profoundly affects physical properties (boiling point, solubility) through intermolecular forces, and chemical reactivity by creating electrophilic ($\delta+$) and nucleophilic ($\delta-$) sites. The 'like dissolves like' rule predicts solubility: polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents, nonpolar in nonpolar. Understanding polarity enables prediction of molecular behavior in chemical and physical contexts.
